title: AI for Science Just Had Its ChatGPT Moment (and Scientists Aren't Extinct)

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I want to ask you a few questions. Why is it dark at night? Why do we dream? What is consciousness? What existed before the universe? Do you know answers to all these questions? I don't.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And that's why AI and science is such a big deal right now. And we just got a few incredible proofs of this. Curious?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Hi, my name is Ksenia, welcome to Attention Span. For almost all of human history, these were the kind of questions we thought are unsolvable. The mysteries so vast that we just accepted we might never know the answers.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But here's the thing, we are currently living through a massive revolution in science that is about to change the timeline of discovery forever. And it's not because AI is going to replace human scientists and make us obsolete, it's about radical, mind-bending acceleration. And we just had a week of breakthroughs that proved it.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Let's start with the absolute bombshell that dropped from OpenAI. For decades, mathematicians have been stumped by a famous problem called the unit distance problem, proposed by the legendary Paul Erdos.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The prevailing belief among the world's smartest minds was that a simple square grid pattern was the absolute best way to maximize the number of unit distance pairs in a plane.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Well, an internal general purpose reasoning model from OpenAI just completely disproved that long-standing conjecture. Do you think it ran numbers? Yes, it did. But it also did much more.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It brought incredibly sophisticated, unexpected concepts from algebraic number theory, things like infinite class field towers and Golod-Shafarevich theory, and applied them to a completely different area of geometry. Fields medalist Tim Gowers called the proof...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: the first really clear example of AI solving not just an unsolved math problem but a really well-known unsolved math problem.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And that wasn't even the only massive news. On May 19th, the very prestigious magazine Nature published a historic trio of peer-reviewed papers showing that AI is breaking boundaries in biology and coding too.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Google DeepMind unveiled Co-scientist, a multi-agent system that acts as a supercharged brainstorming partner. It uses a tournament-style of thinking where different virtual sub-agents propose ideas, critique each other, and rank their hypotheses using the same Elo rating system we use for chess players.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It proposed 30 drug candidates for acute myeloid leukemia, which were actually validated in real wet lab cell lines. At the exact same time, a nonprofit lab called Future House published Robin.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Robin was tasked with finding a treatment for dry age-related macular degeneration, the leading cause of blindness.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It autonomously scanned the literature, identified a biological pathway, and proposed a drug called Reposidil. The crazy part, Reposidil had never been suggested for this condition before.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And to tie it all together, Google and Harvard published ERA, Empirical Research Assistance. ERA is an AI designed to write the highly specialized, high-performance code scientists use to run simulations.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It designed forecasting models for COVID-19 hospitalizations that actually outperformed the CDC's own prediction models.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I know one of the first thoughts might be, 'Oh no, AI is building AI researchers that will eliminate the need to have human researchers and scientists.'

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But if you look closely, these systems are built as co-scientists. They operate in a lab-in-the-loop framework. They cannot run the physical experiments without us, and more importantly, they cannot decide what questions are actually worth asking.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That part is still entirely, beautifully human. This whole worry about AI running out of things for humans to do reminds me of a personal memory from my own career.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Years ago, I was a editor in chief of a popular platform called The Question. It was a site where anyone could ask anything, and our job was to find the absolute best, most qualified experts to answer them.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: When we first launched, I was incredibly stressed. I used to wake up in the middle of the night staring at the ceiling, gripped by this bizarre, irrational fear. I was terrified that one day, people would just run out of questions.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I thought, 'What if we answer everything? What if there is nothing left to ask?'

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But guess what? We never ran out of questions. In fact, the opposite happened. Every time an expert wrote a brilliant, definitive answer, it didn't close the conversation. It opened ten new ones.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: A good answer does not satisfy curiosity. It ignites it. It gives you the vocabulary, the context, and the imagination to ask something even deeper, something you could not have even conceived of the day before.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: And that is the fundamental truth about human curiosity. We will never run out of questions. When people worry that AI will automate science and make human researchers obsolete, they are assuming that science has a finish line. They think that there is a box of mysteries, and once the AI ticks them all off, the scientist can go home.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: But science is not a box. It's an infinite unfolding landscape. AI is not draining the well of discovery. Quite the opposite. It's giving us a deeper bucket to reach the water we've never been able to touch.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Right now, the real tragedy of science is time. Think about a brilliant researcher...

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: They spend their entire 40-year career chasing the answer to one, maybe two incredibly specific puzzles.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: They spend decades writing code, waiting for slow physical simulations to run, hand-screening thousands of chemical compounds, let's say.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That is heroic work, but it's also agonizingly slow.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: This is where a system like Era is a game changer.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Writing scientific software is a massive bottleneck. Biologists and physicists often have to spend months acting as mediocre software developers, debugging code just to test a single hypothesis.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Era collapses that timeline from months or years into mere hours or days.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: The AI handles the algorithmic logistics, allowing the scientist to focus on the actual science.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It is the same story in materials science, which is a massive bottleneck for solving climate change. Finding a new material for carbon capture or a solid-state battery electrolyte traditionally takes a decade of trial and error.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Startups like Orbital Materials and Cosmos AI are now using AI to design materials from the atomic level in a fraction of the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: There are companies like Axiom Math working on solving hard math problems. And more incredibly smart people are starting to leave the world of slow academic cycles and closed labs to build systems designed for acceleration.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Thanks to AI, they now can do that.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: AI does not replace the scientist's judgment. It amplifies it.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: It is our chance to stop spending a lifetime on a single bottleneck and start solving humanity's most pressing crises in weeks.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: So what does the future actually look like? It looks like a world where we actively encourage people to ask more, bolder, and more ambitious questions.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: If you are a young researcher today, you don't have to shy away from an incredibly complex, high-risk problem because you are afraid it will eat up ten years of your life with no guarantee of success.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: You can take on the impossible questions because with AI supporting the infrastructure of science, we have entered a time when no question will be structurally unsolvable.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: We can dream bigger. We can ask how to reverse cellular aging. We can fight cancer. We can learn how to stop climate change. How to build perfectly stable nuclear fusion reactors. How to clean microplastics from our oceans, knowing we finally have the computational horsepower to actually find the answers.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That is the absolute beauty of this transition.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: AI isn't the end of science. It is the beginning of its most creative, open-ended era.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: We are not building machines to think for us. We are building machines to help us think further than we ever thought possible.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Father. Thank you, Demis Hassabis, for inspiring me to record this video.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: That is all for today. Thank you for watching Attention Spen.

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: Let's keep this conversation going in the comments. If you had an AI co-scientist by your side today, if you dream about having, doing more science projects, what unsolvable questions would you want to ask first?

full_transcript.speaker: Ksenia Se
full_transcript.text: I'll see you next week.

highlights: OpenAI's reasoning model recently solved the decades-old unit distance problem by applying sophisticated concepts from algebraic number theory to geometry, marking a major milestone in AI's ability to tackle famous unsolved math problems.

Specialized AI systems like Co-scientist and Robin have begun identifying novel drug candidates for leukemia and blindness by autonomously scanning literature and ranking hypotheses, significantly speeding up the early stages of medical research.

The development of AI-driven research assistants is collapsing scientific timelines from months to hours by automating the creation of high-performance code and simulations, allowing researchers to focus on discovery rather than software development.

AI functions as a "co-scientist" in a lab-in-the-loop framework, meaning it handles complex calculations and data processing while humans remain responsible for the essential task of deciding which questions are worth asking.

We are entering an era where no question is structurally unsolvable, shifting the focus of science from slow manual labor to solving massive global challenges like climate change and nuclear fusion through unprecedented computational horsepower.
summary:
Ksenia Se discusses how artificial intelligence is triggering a massive revolution in scientific discovery, shifting once-unsolvable mysteries into the realm of the attainable. She highlights several recent breakthroughs as evidence: an OpenAI reasoning model solving Paul Erdos’s "unit distance problem" in mathematics, and a series of Nature-published papers featuring AI systems like Google DeepMind’s "Co-scientist" and Future House’s "Robin." These systems have already identified validated drug candidates for leukemia and proposed novel treatments for blindness. Additionally, tools like Google and Harvard’s "ERA" are accelerating research by automating the complex coding required for scientific simulations, significantly outperforming traditional models in fields like epidemiology.

A central theme of the transcript is the concept of the "lab-in-the-loop" framework, where AI serves as a "co-scientist" rather than a replacement for human researchers. Se argues that while AI can handle algorithmic logistics and process vast amounts of data at incredible speeds, humans remain essential for deciding which questions are worth asking. She counters the fear of human obsolescence by asserting that science is an infinite landscape; rather than "finishing" science, AI provides a "deeper bucket" to reach previously inaccessible truths. By collapsing research timelines from decades to days, AI allows scientists to move past tedious bottlenecks and focus on high-stakes challenges like climate change, cellular aging, and clean energy.

Ultimately, the transcript frames this era as the beginning of a more creative and ambitious chapter for humanity. With the "computational horsepower" of AI supporting the infrastructure of science, high-risk problems that were once avoided due to time constraints are now solvable. Se concludes that AI is not intended to think for us, but to empower us to think further than ever before, turning the pursuit of knowledge into an open-ended dialogue where every answer ignites ten new, deeper questions.
description:
In one week: OpenAI cracked the Erdős unit distance conjecture. Google Co-Scientist proposed 30 drug candidates validated in wet labs. FutureHouse Robin found a new blindness treatment. Here's what it means.
This is what Demis Hassabis has always dreamed about, and this episode was inspired by listening to him and talking with him. He said he has never lost his curiosity and keeps asking questions.
I discuss why it matters so much when AI becomes your co-scientist.

🔗 Links Mentioned
DeepMind Co-Scientist  –  https://deepmind.google/blog/co-scientist-a-multi-agent-ai-partner-to-accelerate-research
FutureHouse Robin (paper)  –  https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.13400
Google + Harvard ERA  –  https://seas.harvard.edu/news/ai-system-automates-coding-scientific-research
Orbital Materials  –  https://orbitalmaterials.com/
CuspAI  –  https://www.cuspai.com/
Axiom Math  –  https://www.axiommath.ai/
Interview with Carina Hong from Axiom Math https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3qGBMkgR6Ko


📖 Related reading on turingpost.com:
→ What AI Is Missing for Real Reasoning — Carina Hong, Axiom Math: https://www.turingpost.com/p/carina
→ AI Agents Vocabulary — how multi-agent systems like Co-Scientist work: https://www.turingpost.com/p/agentsvocabulary
→ Reasoning Models Explained — o1, DeepSeek-R1, and beyond: https://www.turingpost.com/p/reasoningmodels
Attention Span is here to show you AI isn’t magic  –  sometimes it’s just compressing 40 years of work into 40 hours.

👉 Interviews: /@realturingpost

topics: AI's role in accelerating scientific research and discovery
Solving long-standing mathematical problems like the Erdős unit distance conjecture
AI breakthroughs in biology and drug discovery (e.g., Co-scientist and Robin)
Automation of scientific software development and research simulations (ERA)
The collaborative future of human researchers and AI co-scientists
